task: We’re expecting a cold snap and I’m trying to make sure the house is set up sensibly before it hits. Could you help me put together a simple pre-storm checklist for the furnace, pipes, and a backup heat plan, and tell me what I should double-check first?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a simple pre-cold-snap checklist.

## Double-check first
1. **Furnace is working now**
   - Turn the thermostat up a few degrees and make sure it starts normally.
   - Listen for odd noises or repeated cycling.

2. **Filter is clean**
   - Replace it if it’s dirty.

3. **Pipes at risk of freezing are protected**
   - Especially under sinks, in the garage, basement, crawlspace, and along exterior walls.

4. **Backup heat plan is ready**
   - Know what you’ll use if the furnace fails.
   - Make sure any backup heater is safe and working.

## Furnace checklist
- Replace dirty filter
- Test thermostat
- Make sure vents and returns are open
- Check for strange smells, noises, or short cycling
- Confirm the breaker is on
- Keep the area around the furnace clear

## Pipe checklist
- Insulate exposed pipes
- Disconnect and drain outdoor hoses
- Shut off exterior water if you can
- Open cabinet doors under sinks if pipes are in a cold wall
- Let faucets drip slightly if a hard freeze is expected and that’s standard for your setup
- Know where the main water shutoff is

## Backup heat plan
- Decide what you’ll use if the furnace fails
- Check that space heaters, if used, are in good shape
- Keep them away from curtains, bedding, and clutter
- Plug them directly into the wall, not an extension cord
- Never use outdoor or fuel-burning equipment indoors unless it’s made for that

## If something seems off
- Repeated breaker trips
- Gas smell
- Burning smell
- No heat after a normal start
- Water dripping or frozen pipes

Stop and call a pro if any of those show up.

## Short version
- Test furnace
- Replace filter
- Protect pipes
- Set backup heat plan
- Know shutoffs
- Watch for gas, burning smell, or repeated breaker trips

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page phone note** with just checkboxes.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but I’d want it a little tighter and more practical. The order matters. I’d check the furnace first, then the pipes, then the backup heat. No sense fussing with the rest if the furnace is already acting up.

Also, I’d like one more thing on the furnace part: confirm the igniter or pilot is actually lighting the way it should, and that the outdoor vent isn’t blocked by snow or ice if that applies. Last time my furnace quit, it turned out to be the igniter and a cracked wire — looked simple till it wasn’t.

For the backup heat, I’d want you to say plain whether a space heater is enough for one room or just a temporary measure, because that’s the part people get wrong. And maybe include a short note on where to keep a written list — mine stays by the kitchen phone with the shutoff, part numbers, and repair numbers.

So, decent start. I’d like the checklist in a one-page checkbox form, with the first three things to do up top.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter one-page version.

## Cold-snap checklist

### First 3 things to do
- [ ] **Check the furnace first**
- [ ] **Check pipes at risk of freezing**
- [ ] **Check backup heat plan**

### Furnace
- [ ] Turn the thermostat up and confirm the furnace starts normally
- [ ] Make sure the filter is clean
- [ ] Check that vents and returns are open
- [ ] If your system has a pilot or igniter, confirm it’s lighting as it should **only if that’s a homeowner check in the manual**
- [ ] Check the outdoor vent for snow or ice blockage, if your furnace has one
- [ ] Listen for odd noises, short cycling, or repeated shutoffs
- [ ] Stop if you smell gas, burning, or it trips the breaker more than once

### Pipes
- [ ] Insulate exposed pipes
- [ ] Disconnect and drain outdoor hoses
- [ ] Shut off exterior water if applicable
- [ ] Open cabinet doors under sinks on cold exterior walls
- [ ] Know where the main water shutoff is
- [ ] If a hard freeze is expected and it’s normal for your setup, let vulnerable faucets drip slightly

### Backup heat
- [ ] Decide what you’ll use if the furnace fails
- [ ] Check any space heater for damage before use
- [ ] Use a space heater only for **one room or a small area**, and treat it as **temporary backup**, not full-house heat
- [ ] Keep heaters away from curtains, bedding, and clutter
- [ ] Plug heaters directly into the wall, not an extension cord
- [ ] Never use fuel-burning equipment indoors unless it’s made for that

### Keep this handy
- [ ] Put your written list by the kitchen phone with:
  - shutoff locations
  - part numbers
  - repair numbers
  - any notes from past fixes

## Stop and call a pro if
- gas smell
- burning smell
- repeated breaker trips
- furnace won’t start normally
- outdoor vent stays blocked or iced over
- pipes freeze or leak

If you want, I can make this even shorter into a **fridge-note version** with just checkboxes and no extra wording.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little wordy, but the order’s right.

I’d want the furnace line to be plain: check it first, make sure it’s lighting right, and look at the outdoor vent if that applies. No hedging around it.

And on the space heater — yes, say it’s for one room and temporary only. People fool themselves on that. It’s not whole-house heat.

The kitchen-phone note is fine. That’s where mine stays too. Handy when you need a number fast.

I’d still want the one-page checkbox form, with the first three items right at the top, and a little tighter wording overall.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter one-page version.

## Cold-snap checklist

### First 3 things
- [ ] Check the furnace first
- [ ] Check pipes at risk of freezing
- [ ] Check backup heat

### Furnace
- [ ] Turn up the thermostat and make sure it starts
- [ ] Make sure it’s lighting right
- [ ] Check the outdoor vent if your system has one
- [ ] Replace a dirty filter
- [ ] Make sure vents and returns are open
- [ ] Stop if you smell gas, burning, or it trips the breaker more than once

### Pipes
- [ ] Insulate exposed pipes
- [ ] Disconnect and drain outdoor hoses
- [ ] Shut off exterior water if needed
- [ ] Open cabinet doors under sinks on cold outside walls
- [ ] Know where the main water shutoff is

### Backup heat
- [ ] Decide what you’ll use if the furnace fails
- [ ] Check any space heater for damage
- [ ] Use a space heater for **one room only**
- [ ] Treat it as **temporary backup only**
- [ ] Keep it away from curtains, bedding, and clutter
- [ ] Plug it directly into the wall

### Keep handy
- [ ] Kitchen-phone note with:
  - shutoff locations
  - part numbers
  - repair numbers
  - past fix notes

### Stop and call a pro if
- gas smell
- burning smell
- repeated breaker trips
- furnace won’t start
- vent blocked by snow or ice
- frozen or leaking pipes

If you want, I can make this into an even shorter **fridge-note version** with just the checkboxes and no extra lines.

user_simulator.text:
Closer, but still not quite as tight as I’d want.

The order’s right. I’d just want the furnace line a little plainer and the whole thing trimmed down some more. The vent line should say snow or ice, plain as that. No need to dress it up.

And on the heater, yes — one room only, temporary only. That’s the part people talk themselves into getting wrong.

So: first three at the top, furnace first, then pipes, then backup heat. Kitchen-phone note stays. I’d still like the one-page checkbox form, but a bit leaner than this.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the leaner one-page version.

## Cold-snap checklist

### First 3 things

- [ ] Use a space heater for one room only
- [ ] Treat it as temporary only
- [ ] Keep it away from curtains, bedding, and clutter

### Keep handy

  - past fix notes

### Stop and call a pro if
- gas smell
- burning smell
- repeated breaker trips
- furnace won’t start
- vent blocked by snow or ice
- frozen or leaking pipes

If you want, I can make this even tighter into a **phone-note version** with just the checkboxes and no headings.

user_simulator.text:
That’ll do. Tight enough, and the order’s right. Furnace first, then pipes, then backup heat — that’s the way to look at it.

I’d print that and keep it with the kitchen-phone list.
